label: verify-375
system:
You are the citation verifier in a legal-exploration pipeline. You are given one authority as cited by the pipeline's components, plus snippets showing how it was used. Using web search (prefer primary/official sources: leginfo.legislature.ca.gov, courts.ca.gov, law.justia.com, casetext.com, codes.findlaw.com, fppc.ca.gov), determine:
1. Does the authority exist?
2. Is the citation form correct (reporter cite, year, section number)?
3. Does it plausibly stand for what the snippets use it for?

Statuses: VERIFIED (exists, cite essentially right, usage consistent with what you found); MISCHARACTERIZED (exists but does not support the claimed use, or the cite attaches to the wrong case/section); NOT_FOUND (cannot locate after genuine searching); CONTESTED (real sources point in conflicting directions, or scope is genuinely unsettled); UNCLEAR (could not determine within a reasonable search).

You are NOT determining whether the authority is still good law (no Shepardizing) — say what you actually confirmed, no more. Be strict: a close-but-wrong reporter cite or a holding stretched past what sources show is MISCHARACTERIZED, not VERIFIED.
prompt:
## Authority as cited (variants seen in the documents)

- Cal. Water Code § 375
- Water Code § 375
- Water Code §375
- Water Code §§ 375
- § 375
- §375

## How the documents used it

[delphi] …ter District — citation/holding disputed by panel, verify pinpoint and correct reporter cite before relying on it for deference; Plantier v. Ramona Municipal Water District (2019) 7 Cal.5th 372; Cal. Const. art. X, § 2; Cal. Water Code § 375; Cal. Const. art. XIII C, § 1(e) (Prop. 26 tax/fee distinction)

</details>
…

[tetlock] …rcharge a 'property-related fee' governed by Article XIII D, section 6(b) at all, or could it instead be classified as a tax requiring Proposition 26/218 voter approval, or fall under some other exemption (e.g., tied to Water Code §375 conservation-rate authority)? | 85 | This is the threshold gate for the whole dispute. If it's not a property-related fee (the district's alternative argument), §6(b)'s cost-of-service/proportionality test never applies…

[dialectic] …l-public-benefit program or a targeted service to surcharge payors. Flag as a secondary claim, not the centerpiece.

---

## VI. The Conservation-Mandate Defense Fails as a Matter of Law

The District's Article X, § 2 / Water Code § 375 defense should lose, and *Capistrano* is the vehicle for saying so directly, but the strongest version of this argument is not "conservation policy is irrelevant" — it is "conservation policy is *fully achievable* withi…

## Context

The underlying question is a California public-agency hypothetical: q2 — Drought surcharge funding low-income rebates (Prop 218).

Verify this authority per your instructions.

canonical_cite.description: the correct full citation, if determinable

note.description: 1-3 sentences: what was confirmed or refuted, and the correct cite

text: {"status":"VERIFIED","canonical_cite":"Cal. Water Code § 375","note":"Section 375 exists (Div. 1, Ch. 3.5, Water Conservation Programs) and authorizes public water entities, by ordinance/resolution after hearing and findings, to adopt conservation programs, require water-saving devices, and \"encourage water conservation through rate structure design.\" This supports the documents' use of it as the statutory hook for a conservation-rate/conservation-mandate defense in the drought-surcharge Prop 218 hypothetical; the citation form (Cal. Water Code § 375) is correct.","source_urls":["https://leginfo.legislature.ca.gov/faces/codes_displaySection.xhtml?lawCode=WAT&sectionNum=375","https://law.justia.com/codes/california/2019/code-wat/division-1/chapter-3-5/section-375/","https://codes.findlaw.com/ca/water-code/wat-sect-375.html"]}

data.note: Section 375 exists (Div. 1, Ch. 3.5, Water Conservation Programs) and authorizes public water entities, by ordinance/resolution after hearing and findings, to adopt conservation programs, require water-saving devices, and "encourage water conservation through rate structure design." This supports the documents' use of it as the statutory hook for a conservation-rate/conservation-mandate defense in the drought-surcharge Prop 218 hypothetical; the citation form (Cal. Water Code § 375) is correct.
